subject.text: Why is readiness reified as a committed tuple instead of a synchronous call?

data.text: My intuition says a callback would be simpler.

data.instruction: Author a bounded scene over the learner's root material: 3-4 question knots and a closing instruction.

scope.subject: Why is readiness reified as a committed tuple instead of a synchronous call?
scope.root: My intuition says a callback would be simpler.

questions: What exactly is meant by "Why is readiness reified as a committed tuple instead of a synchronous call?", and which problem does it exist to solve?
Which normative rules or invariants govern it, and where are they stated?
What happens when it fails, stalls, or is absent — what does the honest failure look like?
How does it connect to the rest of the machine — what feeds it and what consumes it?

result.title: Understanding: Why is readiness reified as a committed tuple instead of a synchronous call?
result.purpose: Settle what "Why is readiness reified as a committed tuple instead of a synchronous call?" means, which rules govern it, and how it behaves at its boundaries.

data.text: Reified readiness keeps the activation chain on the log: descriptors are woken by a committed sys.knot.ready tuple, so replay and audit see the full chain.

data.answer: Reified readiness keeps the activation chain on the log: descriptors are woken by a committed sys.knot.ready tuple, so replay and audit see the full chain.

result.title: Understanding: Which normative rules or invariants govern it, and where are they stated?
result.purpose: Settle what "Which normative rules or invariants govern it, and where are they stated?" means, which rules govern it, and how it behaves at its boundaries.

data.text: The invariants live in Vol. 02 §5: readiness is reified before reset, committed before any descriptor runs, and descriptors are activated only by the committed tuple.

data.answer: The invariants live in Vol. 02 §5: readiness is reified before reset, committed before any descriptor runs, and descriptors are activated only by the committed tuple.
